---
title: "Question2"
author: "NF Katzke"
date: "November 2021"
# date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
bibliography: Tex/ref.bib       # Do not edit: Keep this naming convention and location.
output:
  pagedown::html_paged:
    # template: wp_paged.html
    # css: ['wp.css', 'wp-fonts.css', 'wp-page.css']
    css: ["Template/default-fonts-Texevier.css", "Template/default-page-Texevier.css", "Template/default-Texevier.css"]
    csl: Template/harvard-stellenbosch-university.csl # referencing format used.
    template: ["Template/paged-Texevier.html"]

    toc: true
    # change to true for a self-contained document, but it'll be a litte slower for Pandoc to render
    self_contained: TRUE
abstract: |
    This is an abstract. Much can be written here. Uncomment this line to go without an abstract.
    Abstracts have no spaces, but can have bullets.

    Bullets can be created as follows

    + You can add bullets, but do not add colons please.

    + Line breaks are also not permitted.

---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE)

# 1. SET THE TOOLBOX FIRST
.libPaths("C:/R-local-libs")

# 2. LOAD LIBRARIES (pacman will now find them locally)
pacman::p_load(modelsummary, gt, knitr, kableExtra, tidyverse, pagedown, bookdown)

# 3. SET THE DESK (Absolute paths to prevent missing file errors)
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = "C:/datsci exam 1/24954578/Question2")
setwd("C:/datsci exam 1/24954578/Question2")

gc()
rm(list =ls())

# 4. LOAD DATA (Using absolute paths)
Baby_Names <- read_rds("C:/datsci exam 1/24954578/data/US_Baby_names/Baby_Names_By_US_State.rds") %>%
    rename_with(tolower) %>%
    mutate(name = str_to_title(name), gender = toupper(gender))

national_names <- Baby_Names %>%  
        group_by(name, year, gender)

hbo_titles <- read_rds("C:/datsci exam 1/24954578/data/US_Baby_names/HBO_titles.rds") %>%  
    rename_with(tolower) 

hbo_credits <- read_rds("C:/datsci exam 1/24954578/data/US_Baby_names/HBO_credits.rds")  %>%
    mutate(across(where(is.character), ~ iconv(., to = "UTF-8", sub = "byte"))) %>%
    rename_with(tolower)

hbo <- left_join(hbo_credits, hbo_titles, "id") %>% 
         select(-c(imdb_votes, tmdb_score, role, age_certification, runtime, imdb_id))

charts <- read_rds("C:/datsci exam 1/24954578/data/US_Baby_names/charts.rds") %>% 
       rename_with(~ tolower(gsub("[ -]", "_", .x))) %>%
       mutate(date = as_date(date), year = year(date))

# 5. SOURCE SCRIPTS
list.files("C:/datsci exam 1/24954578/Question2/code", full.names = TRUE, recursive = TRUE) %>% 
    as.list() %>% 
    walk(~source(.))
```

\newpage

# Introduction

# Persistence analysis
```{r}
persistence_plot <- run_persistence_analysis(Baby_Names)
persistence_plot
```

# Popularity surges

```{r}

my_static_plot <- build_pop_culture_plot(Baby_Names, charts, hbo)
# Render it interactively
interactive_plot <- ggplotly(my_static_plot, tooltip = "text")
interactive_plot

```
 This plot shows the names for which the year-on-year growth has doubled.
 An observation is coloured red if the name  doubled in a two range of the release rate of a tv show or movie and in coloured blue if the name doubled in the a two range since a artist reached there best position on the Top 100. This clearly illustrates how quickly pop culture affects Baby_Names. Looking at the left-bottom we see Zendaya is name that has grown and has played in many movies recently.


# Density
```{r}
famous_names <- c("Miley", "Billie", "Drake", "Rihanna", "Mariah", "Arya", "Zendaya", "Khaleesi")

fame_plot <- plot_fame_density(Baby_Names, charts, hbo, famous_names)

fame_plot
```

# Genres
```{r}
hbo_genres_plot <- plot_hbo_genres(Baby_Names, hbo)

hbo_genres_plot
```
